meta.name: Onigiri (Japanese Rice Balls) — Classic Base + Filling Options
meta.subtitle: Hand-formed seasoned rice triangles with nori and customizable fillings

source.name: ssa-core
notes: Canonical SSA catalog recipe.
Designed for deterministic planning: base recipe + optional filling modules.
Includes safe-handling notes for rice and fillings.

yield.notes: Makes 4 medium onigiri (about 100–120 g each), depending on shaping.

signals.notes: Freeze plain/salted onigiri well; mayo-based fillings freeze poorly.

tasteProfile.balanceNotes: Salted rice + umami filling. Add furikake for extra savory depth.

notes: Allergens depend on fillings and seasonings (furikake, tuna mayo, soy sauce).
For gluten-free, use GF tamari and verify furikake/seasonings are GF.

ingredients.label: Japanese short-grain rice (or sushi rice)
ingredients.notes: About 1 1/2 cups dry. Short-grain gives best stickiness and shape.

ingredients.label: Water (for cooking rice)
ingredients.notes: About 1 3/4 cups; adjust to your rice brand and cooker markings.

ingredients.label: Fine salt
ingredients.notes: About 1 tsp. Used for seasoning rice and/or hands during shaping.

ingredients.label: Nori sheets
ingredients.notes: Cut into strips to wrap each onigiri (prevents sticky hands while eating).

ingredients.label: Furikake (Japanese rice seasoning)
ingredients.notes: Optional: mix into rice or sprinkle on outside.

ingredients.label: Umeboshi (pickled plum)
ingredients.notes: Classic filling; salty-sour and helps with keeping quality.

ingredients.label: Mayonnaise
ingredients.notes: About 2 Tbsp; for tuna mayo filling.

ingredients.label: Soy sauce
ingredients.notes: About 1 tsp; for tuna mayo seasoning or brushing.

ingredients.label: Cooked salmon flakes
ingredients.notes: Use leftover cooked salmon or canned salmon; flake finely.

steps.title: Rinse the rice
instructions: Place rice in a bowl and cover with water.
Swirl gently, then drain. Repeat 3–5 times until water is mostly clear.
Drain thoroughly in a fine mesh strainer for 2–3 minutes.

steps.title: Cook the rice
instructions: Cook the rinsed rice with measured water in a rice cooker, or in a pot with a tight lid.
If using a pot: bring to a boil, cover, reduce to low, cook 12 minutes; then rest off heat 10 minutes.
Fluff gently with a paddle/spoon to release steam without mashing grains.
checks: Grains cling lightly and hold shape

steps.title: Prepare optional fillings
instructions: Umeboshi: pit if needed; keep whole or chop.
Tuna mayo: mix drained tuna + mayonnaise + a splash of soy sauce (optional).
Salmon: flake cooked salmon; optionally mix with a pinch of salt or sesame.
checks: Fillings are ready in small portions

steps.title: Season rice for shaping
instructions: While rice is warm (not piping hot), lightly salt it or salt your hands/water for shaping.
Optional: mix furikake into the rice for seasoned onigiri.

steps.title: Shape onigiri (triangle or round)
instructions: Prepare a small bowl of water with a pinch of salt. Wet hands lightly to prevent sticking.
Scoop about 1/2 cup warm rice into your palm, make a shallow well, and add 1–2 tsp filling (optional).
Cover filling with rice, then shape into a firm triangle (or round) by gently pressing with both hands.
Repeat for remaining rice. Avoid compressing too hard—aim for cohesive but tender.

steps.title: Wrap with nori and finish
instructions: Cut nori into strips or rectangles and wrap around each onigiri just before eating.
Optional: sprinkle outside with furikake or sesame seeds.

pairings.notes: Onigiri is a portable starch; pair with soup, pickles, or a simple protein for balance.

notes: Best eaten same day. Rice hardens when chilled; wrap tightly to reduce drying.
Re-warm gently (microwave 20–40 seconds) and add nori after reheating.
Freeze plain salted onigiri (no mayo-based fillings). Wrap tightly in plastic, then bag.
Thaw in fridge or microwave from frozen; add nori after reheating.

planner.weeknightNotes: Use leftover rice or rice cooker batch. Make 6–8 onigiri for lunches.

notes: Food safety: plain salted onigiri is safer for room-temp carrying than mayo-based fillings. Keep cold fillings chilled and consume within safe time windows.
If rice is too hot to handle, let it cool 5–8 minutes; shaping works best while still warm.
For crisp nori, wrap right before eating; pre-wrapped nori can become chewy.
Skip fillings; season rice lightly with salt and shape.
Great for dipping into soup or serving with grilled fish.
Mix tuna + mayo + soy sauce. Keep chilled and consume promptly.
Not ideal for freezing.
Use 1/2 umeboshi per onigiri for stronger flavor.
Umeboshi helps keeping quality due to acidity and salt.
Use flaked cooked salmon. Optional sesame mixed in.
Freezes better than tuna mayo.

summary: Classic Japanese onigiri made with sticky short-grain rice, a touch of salt, optional nori wrap, and easy mix-and-match fillings like umeboshi, tuna mayo, or salmon flakes.

foodSafety: Cool rice slightly before shaping to avoid burns; keep hands and tools clean.
If packing for later, cool quickly, wrap tightly, and refrigerate; avoid leaving mayo-based fillings at room temperature.
Reheat chilled onigiri briefly to soften rice; wrap nori after reheating.
allergenNotes: Allergens depend on fillings (tuna, mayo/egg, soy sauce) and seasonings (furikake).
Verify gluten-free needs for soy sauce and seasonings.
